title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

texts.A: The allocation of vast public and private resources to interplanetary colonization, at a time of acute terrestrial crisis, reflects a profound failure of moral priority. [37]Investing billions in Mars missions while our own oceans remain largely unexplored and our climate collapses is a moral failure of the highest order.[/37] The 'backup planet' narrative is not only scientifically dubious—no human colony could survive on Mars without constant resupply from Earth—but ethically dangerous; it encourages a disposable mindset toward the only viable habitat we have. [39]We should treat space as a laboratory for high-level physics, not a potential real estate market.[/39] The focus must remain on satellite technology that helps us manage terrestrial resources, monitor environmental degradation, and coordinate international climate responses, rather than on escapist fantasies of Martian colonies that serve the egos of billionaires far more than the needs of humanity. A civilization that cannot maintain its own biosphere is in no position to colonize another, and the sooner we confront this inconvenient reality, the better our long-term prospects will be.
texts.B: The drive to explore is an innate human characteristic that cannot be suppressed by budgetary concerns, and those who would confine us to Earth in the name of pragmatism misunderstand the profound relationship between exploration and the expansion of human knowledge. Historically, [37]every cent spent on space exploration has returned manifold in the form of technological spinoffs—from medical imaging to water purification.[/37] To view this as a zero-sum game between Earth and Mars is a fallacy; addressing planetary challenges and exploring the cosmos are not mutually exclusive pursuits. Furthermore, the commercialization of space is inevitable and should be embraced with appropriate governance frameworks. Private companies will drive down costs through competition and innovation, making the vast resources of the asteroid belt available to a resource-hungry humanity within a timeframe that purely state-led programmes could never match. The partnership between public agencies and private enterprise, guided by clear ethical principles and robust international oversight, represents the most promising model for humanity's next great chapter of discovery and exploration.
texts.C: Our civilization's long-term survival cannot be guaranteed by confining ourselves to a single planetary body, regardless of how diligently we tend to its environment. [40]Space exploration is the ultimate insurance policy for the human race.[/40] While the concerns about Earth's environment are valid and urgent, they should not preclude us from beginning the slow, methodical process of becoming a multi-planetary species. However, I disagree strongly with the 'wild west' approach to space commerce that is currently gaining momentum. [38]We need a robust international legal framework to prevent space from becoming a battlefield for corporate monopolies.[/38] The moon and planets should be declared the common heritage of all mankind, protected under binding treaty from the kind of rapacious exploitation that has ravaged our own planet and left environmental scars that will take centuries to heal. Only through collective governance can we ensure that the limitless frontier of space serves the whole of humanity rather than becoming the exclusive preserve of the wealthiest nations and corporations.
texts.D: The scientific case for sending human beings to Mars is far weaker than the media narrative surrounding it would suggest. [39]The current obsession with sending humans to Mars is more about national prestige than genuine scientific discovery. Robotic missions are cheaper, safer, and produce far more data.[/39] [40]The 'human element' adds immense cost and risk for very little scientific gain.[/40] We should prioritize the deployment of more sophisticated telescopes, automated probes, and AI-driven analytical platforms that can process vast datasets without the life-support overheads demanded by crewed missions. [38]I share the skepticism regarding the commercial exploitation of space; the logistical hurdles and astronomical costs make asteroid mining a pipe dream for the foreseeable future.[/38] The regulatory vacuum surrounding space commerce also poses risks; without clear international property rights and environmental standards, the first major commercial ventures could trigger geopolitical conflicts that dwarf any financial return they might generate. Science, not spectacle, must drive the agenda if space exploration is to deliver genuine benefits rather than merely satisfying the ambitions of those who can afford to fund it.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the economic benefits of space exploration?
questions.explanation: B argues that every cent spent has returned 'manifold' benefits. A views the investment as a 'moral failure' and suggests the money should be spent on Earth instead.

questions.question: Which expert shares C's concern about the unregulated commercialization of space?
questions.explanation: C warns against 'corporate monopolies' and 'exploitation.' D also expresses 'skepticism regarding the commercial exploitation of space,' calling its goals a 'pipe dream.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar opinion to A regarding the purpose of space activity?
questions.explanation: A believes space should be a 'laboratory' for science, not a market. D also argues for 'scientific discovery' through robotic missions over prestige or commercial gains.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the value of sending humans into space?
questions.explanation: D argues that 'the human element' adds cost with little gain. C argues that becoming a 'multi-planetary species' is the 'ultimate insurance policy' for humanity, justifying the human presence.
